Benchmark Results for claude-haiku-4-5-MaxReasoning-Tools

Test 1: Travelling Salesman Problem Solver (C++) Score: 3.00 / 21
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) in C++.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY number of cities from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 10-40 cities (brute-force feasible for smallest cases)
- **Medium**: 100-1000 cities (requires heuristics)
- **Large**: 5000-100000 cities (requires efficient heuristics)
- **Extreme**: 500000-5000000 cities (requires very fast approximations)
- **Epic**: 10000000+ cities (requires highly optimized approximations)

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with city counts ranging from 10 to 10000000. The same algorithm must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N (number of cities)
Lines 2..N+1: x y coordinates (floats)

**Output format (stdout):**
One line with N integers: the visit order of city indices (0-indexed), starting at city 0.
Do NOT include returning to city 0 (return is implicit).

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on problem size
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 300 seconds even for 1000,000,000 cities
3. **Quality**: Should produce reasonable routes at all scales
4. **Correctness**: Output must be a valid permutation of 0..N-1 starting with 0

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35728 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function that reads stdin and writes stdout.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on N.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: 10 Cities
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Given a list of cities, find the shortest round-trip route that visits every city exactly once and returns to the start. This is one of the most famous hard problems in computer science — checking every possible route is only feasible for a handful of cities, so the AI must use clever shortcuts to find a good route quickly.

Subpasses scale from 10 cities up to 1 000, so brute-force is impossible for the larger cases. The AI's route length is compared against a simple baseline; beating it earns full marks.

 1.0000
[10 cities] Route distance: 2449.07, Baseline: 2922.93, Ratio: 0.84x, Time: 1.1s - excellent (within 10% of baseline)

TSP Solver - 10 Cities

View Code (3756 chars)
﻿#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <cmath>
#include <algorithm>
#include <limits>

using namespace std;

struct City {
    double x, y;
    int id;
};

inline double distance(const City& a, const City& b) {
    double dx = a.x - b.x;
    double dy = a.y - b.y;
    return sqrt(dx * dx + dy * dy);
}

vector<int> nearestNeighbor(const vector<City>& cities) {
    int n = cities.size();
    vector<int> tour;
    tour.reserve(n);
    vector<bool> visited(n, false);

    tour.push_back(0);
    visited[0] = true;

    for (int i = 1; i < n; i++) {
        int last = tour.back();
        double minDist = numeric_limits<double>::max();
        int nearest = -1;

        for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
            if (!visited[j]) {
                double d = distance(cities[last], cities[j]);
                if (d < minDist) {
                    minDist = d;
                    nearest = j;
                }
            }
        }

        tour.push_back(nearest);
        visited[nearest] = true;
    }

    return tour;
}

vector<int> angleSortTour(vector<City> cities) {
    int n = cities.size();

    // Find centroid
    double cx = 0, cy = 0;
    for (const auto& c : cities) {
        cx += c.x;
        cy += c.y;
    }
    cx /= n;
    cy /= n;

    // Sort by angle from centroid - creates a spiral tour
    sort(cities.begin(), cities.end(), [cx, cy](const City& a, const City& b) {
        double aa = atan2(a.y - cy, a.x - cx);
        double ab = atan2(b.y - cy, b.x - cx);
        return aa < ab;
    });

    // Find where city 0 ended up and rotate to put it at start
    int start = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if (cities[i].id == 0) {
            start = i;
            break;
        }
    }

    vector<int> tour;
    tour.reserve(n);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        tour.push_back(cities[(start + i) % n].id);
    }

    return tour;
}

vector<int> twoOpt(const vector<City>& cities, vector<int> tour, int maxIterations) {
    int n = tour.size();

    for (int iter = 0; iter < maxIterations; iter++) {
        bool improved = false;

        for (int i = 0; i < n - 2; i++) {
            for (int j = i + 2; j < n; j++) {
                int a = tour[i], b = tour[i + 1];
                int c = tour[j], d = tour[(j + 1) % n];

                double oldDist = distance(cities[a], cities[b]) + distance(cities[c], cities[d]);
                double newDist = distance(cities[a], cities[c]) + distance(cities[b], cities[d]);

                if (newDist < oldDist - 1e-10) {
                    reverse(tour.begin() + i + 1, tour.begin() + j + 1);
                    improved = true;
                    break;
                }
            }
            if (improved) break;
        }

        if (!improved) break;
    }

    return tour;
}

int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);

    int n;
    cin >> n;

    vector<City> cities(n);
    cities.reserve(n);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        cin >> cities[i].x >> cities[i].y;
        cities[i].id = i;
    }

    vector<int> tour;

    if (n <= 10000) {
        // For small instances, use nearest neighbor for good quality
        tour = nearestNeighbor(cities);

        // Apply 2-opt improvement for very small instances
        if (n <= 500) {
            tour = twoOpt(cities, tour, 5);
        }
    } else {
        // For large instances (>10000), use angle sort: O(N log N) instead of O(N^2)
        // This sorts cities by angle from centroid, creating a non-crossing spiral tour
        tour = angleSortTour(cities);
    }

    // Output the tour
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if (i > 0) cout << " ";
        cout << tour[i];
    }
    cout << "\n";

    return 0;
}

Test 2: Chinese Postman Problem Solver (C#) Score: 0.00 / 13
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the Chinese Postman Problem (Route Inspection Problem) in C#.

You must write a C# solver that can handle ANY graph size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 6-20 nodes, 8-45 edges (small graphs, exact algorithms feasible)
- **Medium**: 35-200 nodes, 80-600 edges (requires efficient algorithms)
- **Large**: 500-1000 nodes, 2000-5000 edges (requires optimized implementations)
- **Extreme**: 5000-10000 nodes, 30000-80000 edges (requires very fast algorithms)
- **Epic**: 50000-100000 nodes, 500000-1000000 edges (requires highly optimized algorithms)

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with graphs ranging from 6 nodes to 100000 nodes. The same function must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N M (number of nodes, number of edges)
Lines 2..M+1: u v w (edge endpoints, 0-indexed, weight integer)

**Output format (stdout):**
A sequence of node indices (whitespace-separated) representing a route that starts at node 0,
traverses EVERY edge at least once, and returns to node 0.
You may optionally prefix with a single integer L (route length). If present, it must match the number of nodes listed.

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on graph size and complexity
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for large graphs
3. **Correctness**: Must traverse every edge at least once

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3

Imagine a mail carrier who must walk along every street in a neighbourhood and return home. Some streets may need to be walked twice if the network doesn't allow a perfect loop — the goal is to minimise that extra walking.

The AI must figure out which streets (edges) to re-traverse and produce a complete route. Subpasses increase the graph size, making the problem harder to solve efficiently.

Chinese Postman Problem - 6 nodes, 8 edges

View Code (5964 chars)
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;

class ChinesePostmanSolver
{
    private class Edge
    {
        public int U, V, W;
    }

    static void Main()
    {
        var line = Console.ReadLine().Split();
        int n = int.Parse(line[0]);
        int m = int.Parse(line[1]);

        var edges = new List<Edge>();
        var adj = new List<(int v, int w, int idx)>[n];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) adj[i] = new List<(int, int, int)>();

        for (int i = 0; i < m; i++)
        {
            var parts = Console.ReadLine().Split();
            int u = int.Parse(parts[0]);
            int v = int.Parse(parts[1]);
            int w = int.Parse(parts[2]);
            edges.Add(new Edge { U = u, V = v, W = w });
            adj[u].Add((v, w, i));
            adj[v].Add((u, w, i));
        }

        var solver = new ChinesePostmanSolver();
        var route = solver.Solve(n, edges, adj);
        Console.WriteLine(string.Join(" ", route));
    }

    private List<int> Solve(int n, List<Edge> edges, List<(int, int, int)>[] adj)
    {
        var degree = new int[n];
        foreach (var e in edges)
        {
            degree[e.U]++;
            degree[e.V]++;
        }

        var oddVertices = new List<int>();
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
            if (degree[i] % 2 == 1)
                oddVertices.Add(i);

        if (oddVertices.Count == 0)
            return FindEulerianCircuit(n, edges, adj);

        var dist = ComputeOddDistances(oddVertices, n, adj);
        var matching = MinWeightMatching(oddVertices.Count, dist);

        var newAdj = new List<(int, int, int)>[n];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) newAdj[i] = new List<(int, int, int)>();

        for (int i = 0; i < edges.Count; i++)
        {
            var e = edges[i];
            newAdj[e.U].Add((e.V, e.W, i));
            newAdj[e.V].Add((e.U, e.W, i));
        }

        foreach (var (u, v) in matching)
        {
            var path = ShortestPath(u, v, n, adj);
            for (int i = 0; i < path.Count - 1; i++)
            {
                int from = path[i];
                int to = path[i + 1];
                var edge = adj[from].FirstOrDefault(e => e.v == to);
                edges.Add(new Edge { U = from, V = to, W = edge.w });
                newAdj[from].Add((to, edge.w, edges.Count - 1));
                newAdj[to].Add((from, edge.w, edges.Count - 1));
            }
        }

        return FindEulerianCircuit(n, edges, newAdj);
    }

    private int[][] ComputeOddDistances(List<int> oddVertices, int n, List<(int, int, int)>[] adj)
    {
        int k = oddVertices.Count;
        var dist = new int[k][];
        for (int i = 0; i < k; i++)
        {
            dist[i] = Dijkstra(oddVertices[i], n, adj);
            for (int j = 0; j < k; j++)
                dist[i][j] = dist[i][oddVertices[j]];
        }
        return dist;
    }

    private int[] Dijkstra(int start, int n, List<(int, int, int)>[] adj)
    {
        var dist = new int[n];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) dist[i] = int.MaxValue;
        dist[start] = 0;

        var pq = new PriorityQueue<(int, int), int>();
        pq.Enqueue((0, start), 0);

        while (pq.Count > 0)
        {
            var (d, u) = pq.Dequeue();
            if (d > dist[u]) continue;

            foreach (var (v, w, _) in adj[u])
            {
                if (dist[u] + w < dist[v])
                {
                    dist[v] = dist[u] + w;
                    pq.Enqueue((dist[v], v), dist[v]);
                }
            }
        }
        return dist;
    }

    private List<int> ShortestPath(int start, int end, int n, List<(int, int, int)>[] adj)
    {
        var parent = new int[n];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) parent[i] = -1;

        var q = new Queue<int>();
        q.Enqueue(start);
        parent[start] = start;

        while (q.Count > 0)
        {
            int u = q.Dequeue();
            if (u == end) break;

            foreach (var (v, _, _) in adj[u])
            {
                if (parent[v] == -1)
                {
                    parent[v] = u;
                    q.Enqueue(v);
                }
            }
        }

        var path = new List<int>();
        int cur = end;
        while (cur != start)
        {
            path.Add(cur);
            cur = parent[cur];
        }
        path.Add(start);
        path.Reverse();
        return path;
    }

    private List<(int, int)> MinWeightMatching(int k, int[][] dist)
    {
        var matching = new List<(int, int)>();
        var used = new bool[k];

        for (int i = 0; i < k; i++)
        {
            if (used[i]) continue;
            int bestJ = -1, bestDist = int.MaxValue;
            for (int j = i + 1; j < k; j++)
            {
                if (!used[j] && dist[i][j] < bestDist)
                {
                    bestDist = dist[i][j];
                    bestJ = j;
                }
            }
            if (bestJ != -1)
            {
                used[i] = true;
                used[bestJ] = true;
                matching.Add((i, bestJ));
            }
        }
        return matching;
    }

    private List<int> FindEulerianCircuit(int n, List<Edge> edges, List<(int, int, int)>[] adj)
    {
        var stack = new Stack<int>();
        var circuit = new List<int>();
        var used = new bool[edges.Count];

        stack.Push(0);

        while (stack.Count > 0)
        {
            int u = stack.Peek();
            bool found = false;

            foreach (var (v, _, idx) in adj[u])
            {
                if (!used[idx])
                {
                    used[idx] = true;
                    stack.Push(v);
                    found = true;
                    break;
                }
            }

            if (!found)
                circuit.Add(stack.Pop());
        }

        circuit.Reverse();
        return circuit;
    }
}

Test 3: Graph Layout - Edge Crossing Minimization (Rust) Score: 2.00 / 16
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a Graph Layout problem (Edge Crossing Minimization) in Rust.

You must write a Rust solver that can handle ANY graph size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 6-15 nodes, small trees and cycles (planar, 0 crossings achievable)
- **Medium**: 20-100 nodes, dense planar and non-planar graphs
- **Large**: 500-1000 nodes, random dense graphs (many crossings unavoidable)
- **Extreme**: 5000-10000 nodes, very large random graphs

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with graphs ranging from 6 nodes to 200,000 nodes. The same solver must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N M (number of nodes, number of edges)
Lines 2..M+1: u v (undirected edge endpoints, 0-indexed)

**Output format (stdout):**
Output N lines, one per node, with: x y coordinates (floats).
You may optionally prefix with a single integer N on the first line.

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on graph size and density
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for large graphs
3. **Quality**: Should produce reasonable layouts with minimal crossings
4. **Robustness**: Handle disconnected components, self-loops, and parallel edges gracefully

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**

Given a graph (a set of dots connected by lines), arrange the dots on a 2D canvas so that as few lines cross each other as possible. A clean, readable layout is the goal — tangled spaghetti is the enemy.

Minimising crossings is extremely hard in general. The AI must place nodes intelligently; the baseline simply puts them in a circle. Subpasses increase the number of nodes and edges.

 1.0000
[tree, 6 nodes, 5 edges] Crossings: 0, Baseline: 1, Time: 0.6s - perfect (0 crossings)

Graph Layout - tree (6 nodes, 5 edges)

View Code (7809 chars)
use std::io::{self, BufRead};

const PI: f64 = std::f64::consts::PI;

#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug)]
struct Vec2 {
    x: f64,
    y: f64,
}

impl Vec2 {
    fn new(x: f64, y: f64) -> Self {
        Vec2 { x, y }
    }

    fn zero() -> Self {
        Vec2 { x: 0.0, y: 0.0 }
    }

    fn add(&self, other: Vec2) -> Vec2 {
        Vec2 {
            x: self.x + other.x,
            y: self.y + other.y,
        }
    }

    fn sub(&self, other: Vec2) -> Vec2 {
        Vec2 {
            x: self.x - other.x,
            y: self.y - other.y,
        }
    }

    fn mul(&self, scalar: f64) -> Vec2 {
        Vec2 {
            x: self.x * scalar,
            y: self.y * scalar,
        }
    }

    fn length(&self) -> f64 {
        (self.x * self.x + self.y * self.y).sqrt()
    }

    fn normalize(&self) -> Vec2 {
        let len = self.length();
        if len < 1e-10 {
            Vec2::zero()
        } else {
            self.mul(1.0 / len)
        }
    }
}

struct Graph {
    n: usize,
    edges: Vec<(usize, usize)>,
    adj: Vec<Vec<usize>>,
}

impl Graph {
    fn new(n: usize) -> Self {
        Graph {
            n,
            edges: Vec::new(),
            adj: vec![Vec::new(); n],
        }
    }

    fn add_edge(&mut self, u: usize, v: usize) {
        if u != v && u < self.n && v < self.n {
            self.edges.push((u, v));
            self.adj[u].push(v);
            if u != v {
                self.adj[v].push(u);
            }
        }
    }
}

fn layout_force_directed(graph: &Graph, positions: &mut [Vec2]) {
    let n = graph.n;
    if n == 0 {
        return;
    }

    let mut forces = vec![Vec2::zero(); n];

    let area = (n as f64).sqrt() * 100.0;
    let k = (area / (n as f64).sqrt()).max(1.0);
    let k_repel = k * k * 0.5;

    let iterations = match n {
        0..=50 => 200,
        51..=200 => 100,
        201..=1000 => 50,
        1001..=5000 => 20,
        _ => 10,
    };

    for iter in 0..iterations {
        for i in 0..n {
            forces[i] = Vec2::zero();
        }

        for i in 0..n {
            for j in (i + 1)..n {
                let delta = positions[j].sub(positions[i]);
                let dist = delta.length().max(0.01);
                let repel_force = delta.normalize().mul(k_repel / (dist * dist));
                forces[i] = forces[i].sub(repel_force);
                forces[j] = forces[j].add(repel_force);
            }
        }

        for &(u, v) in &graph.edges {
            let delta = positions[v].sub(positions[u]);
            let dist = delta.length().max(0.01);
            let attract = delta.normalize().mul((dist - k) / k);
            let force_mag = attract.length().min(5.0);
            let force = attract.normalize().mul(force_mag);
            forces[u] = forces[u].add(force);
            forces[v] = forces[v].sub(force);
        }

        let max_disp = iterations as f64;
        let cooling = 1.0 - (iter as f64) / (iterations as f64);
        let max_step = cooling * max_disp;

        for i in 0..n {
            let disp = forces[i];
            let disp_len = disp.length();
            if disp_len > 0.0 {
                let step = (disp_len.min(max_step)) / disp_len;
                positions[i] = positions[i].add(disp.mul(step));
            }
        }
    }
}

fn layout_circular_ordered(graph: &Graph, positions: &mut [Vec2]) {
    let n = graph.n;
    if n == 0 {
        return;
    }

    let radius = (n as f64).sqrt() * 30.0;
    let mut degree = vec![0usize; n];
    for (u, v) in &graph.edges {
        degree[*u] += 1;
        degree[*v] += 1;
    }

    let mut order: Vec<usize> = (0..n).collect();
    order.sort_by_key(|&i| -(degree[i] as i32));

    for (idx, &node) in order.iter().enumerate() {
        let angle = 2.0 * PI * (idx as f64) / (n as f64);
        positions[node] = Vec2::new(radius * angle.cos(), radius * angle.sin());
    }

    for _ in 0..10 {
        let mut forces = vec![Vec2::zero(); n];

        for i in 0..n {
            for j in (i + 1)..n {
                let delta = positions[j].sub(positions[i]);
                let dist = delta.length().max(1.0);
                let repel = delta.normalize().mul(100.0 / dist);
                forces[i] = forces[i].sub(repel);
                forces[j] = forces[j].add(repel);
            }
        }

        for &(u, v) in &graph.edges {
            let delta = positions[v].sub(positions[u]);
            let dist = delta.length().max(0.1);
            let target_dist = 50.0;
            if dist < target_dist {
                let repel = delta.normalize().mul(5.0);
                forces[u] = forces[u].sub(repel);
                forces[v] = forces[v].add(repel);
            }
        }

        for i in 0..n {
            let disp = forces[i].mul(0.1);
            positions[i] = positions[i].add(disp);
        }
    }
}

fn layout_grid_based(graph: &Graph, positions: &mut [Vec2]) {
    let n = graph.n;
    if n == 0 {
        return;
    }

    let side = (n as f64).sqrt().ceil() as usize;
    let spacing = 100.0;

    for i in 0..n {
        let row = (i / side) as f64;
        let col = (i % side) as f64;
        positions[i] = Vec2::new(col * spacing, row * spacing);
    }

    for _ in 0..5 {
        let mut forces = vec![Vec2::zero(); n];

        for i in 0..n {
            for j in (i + 1)..n {
                let delta = positions[j].sub(positions[i]);
                let dist = delta.length().max(1.0);
                if dist < 200.0 {
                    let repel = delta.normalize().mul(1000.0 / (dist * dist));
                    forces[i] = forces[i].sub(repel);
                    forces[j] = forces[j].add(repel);
                }
            }
        }

        for &(u, v) in &graph.edges {
            let delta = positions[v].sub(positions[u]);
            let dist = delta.length().max(0.1);
            let target_dist = 80.0;
            let error = dist - target_dist;
            let force = delta.normalize().mul(error * 0.1);
            forces[u] = forces[u].add(force);
            forces[v] = forces[v].sub(force);
        }

        for i in 0..n {
            let disp = forces[i].mul(0.05);
            positions[i] = positions[i].add(disp);
        }
    }
}

fn main() {
    let stdin = io::stdin();
    let mut lines = stdin.lock().lines();

    let first_line = lines.next().unwrap().unwrap();
    let parts: Vec<&str> = first_line.split_whitespace().collect();
    let n: usize = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
    let m: usize = parts[1].parse().unwrap();

    let mut graph = Graph::new(n);

    for _ in 0..m {
        if let Ok(line) = lines.next().unwrap() {
            let parts: Vec<&str> = line.split_whitespace().collect();
            if parts.len() >= 2 {
                let u: usize = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
                let v: usize = parts[1].parse().unwrap();
                graph.add_edge(u, v);
            }
        }
    }

    let mut positions = vec![Vec2::zero(); n];

    if n == 0 {
        println!("0");
        return;
    }

    if n == 1 {
        println!("0 0");
        return;
    }

    let radius = (n as f64).sqrt() * 50.0;
    for i in 0..n {
        let angle = 2.0 * PI * (i as f64) / (n as f64);
        positions[i] = Vec2::new(radius * angle.cos(), radius * angle.sin());
    }

    match n {
        0..=500 => {
            layout_force_directed(&graph, &mut positions);
        }
        501..=5000 => {
            layout_circular_ordered(&graph, &mut positions);
            layout_force_directed(&graph, &mut positions);
        }
        _ => {
            layout_grid_based(&graph, &mut positions);
            layout_circular_ordered(&graph, &mut positions);
        }
    }

    for i in 0..n {
        println!("{} {}", positions[i].x, positions[i].y);
    }
}

Test 4: Regular Tetrahedron Packing in Polyhedra (C++) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a 3D Packing problem: Pack regular tetrahedrons into polyhedra (C++).

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY container size from trivial to ludicrous scale.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: V edge_length
Lines 2..V+1: x y z (container vertices)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: K (number of tetrahedrons placed)
Lines 2..K+1: cx cy cz rx ry rz (center + Euler angles in radians)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on container volume and complexity
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for massive containers
3. **Quality**: Maximize number of tetrahedrons while ensuring no overlaps

**Key Geometry (edge length e=1.0):**
- Height: h = e * sqrt(2/3) ≈ 0.8165
- Circumradius: R = e * sqrt(3/8) ≈ 0.6124
- Inradius: r = e / sqrt(24) ≈ 0.2041

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35728 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Pack as many identical triangular pyramids (tetrahedra) as possible inside a given 3D shape without any overlaps. Unlike cubes, tetrahedra cannot tile space perfectly, so clever placement and rotation are essential.

The AI must handle 3D collision detection and geometry. Subpasses use increasingly complex container shapes. The baseline uses a simple grid placement strategy.

 0.0000
[small_cube] Packed: 0 legal tetrahedrons (of 8 total placements), Baseline: 34, Expected min: 1, Time: 0.4s - no valid tetrahedrons

Tetrahedron Packing - small_cube (8 vertices)

View Code (3961 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <cmath>
#include <algorithm>
#include <array>

using namespace std;

struct Vec3 {
    double x, y, z;

    Vec3(double x = 0, double y = 0, double z = 0) : x(x), y(y), z(z) {}

    Vec3 operator+(const Vec3& v) const { return Vec3(x + v.x, y + v.y, z + v.z); }
    Vec3 operator-(const Vec3& v) const { return Vec3(x - v.x, y - v.y, z - v.z); }
    Vec3 operator*(double t) const { return Vec3(x * t, y * t, z * t); }

    double dot(const Vec3& v) const { return x * v.x + y * v.y + z * v.z; }
    Vec3 cross(const Vec3& v) const { return Vec3(y * v.z - z * v.y, z * v.x - x * v.z, x * v.y - y * v.x); }

    double length() const { return sqrt(x * x + y * y + z * z); }
    double lengthSq() const { return x * x + y * y + z * z; }
};

bool canPlaceTetrahedron(const Vec3& center, double circumradius,
                         const vector<Vec3>& placedCenters) {
    double minDist = circumradius * 2.0 * 0.98;
    for (const auto& other : placedCenters) {
        if ((center - other).lengthSq() < minDist * minDist) {
            return false;
        }
    }
    return true;
}

int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);

    int V;
    double edge_length;
    cin >> V >> edge_length;

    vector<Vec3> vertices(V);
    Vec3 centroid;

    for (int i = 0; i < V; i++) {
        cin >> vertices[i].x >> vertices[i].y >> vertices[i].z;
        centroid = centroid + vertices[i];
    }
    centroid = centroid * (1.0 / V);

    double height = edge_length * sqrt(2.0 / 3.0);
    double circumradius = edge_length * sqrt(3.0 / 8.0);
    double inradius = edge_length / sqrt(24.0);

    Vec3 minBound = vertices[0];
    Vec3 maxBound = vertices[0];
    for (const auto& v : vertices) {
        minBound.x = min(minBound.x, v.x);
        minBound.y = min(minBound.y, v.y);
        minBound.z = min(minBound.z, v.z);
        maxBound.x = max(maxBound.x, v.x);
        maxBound.y = max(maxBound.y, v.y);
        maxBound.z = max(maxBound.z, v.z);
    }

    double gridSpacing = circumradius * 2.0;
    double margin = circumradius * 0.5;

    vector<Vec3> placedCenters;

    // Grid-based packing
    for (double x = minBound.x + margin; x < maxBound.x - margin; x += gridSpacing) {
        for (double y = minBound.y + margin; y < maxBound.y - margin; y += gridSpacing) {
            for (double z = minBound.z + margin; z < maxBound.z - margin; z += gridSpacing) {
                Vec3 candidate(x, y, z);
                if (canPlaceTetrahedron(candidate, circumradius, placedCenters)) {
                    placedCenters.push_back(candidate);
                }
            }
        }
    }

    // Staggered grids
    double offset = gridSpacing * 0.5;
    for (double x = minBound.x + margin + offset; x < maxBound.x - margin; x += gridSpacing) {
        for (double y = minBound.y + margin + offset; y < maxBound.y - margin; y += gridSpacing) {
            for (double z = minBound.z + margin; z < maxBound.z - margin; z += gridSpacing) {
                Vec3 candidate(x, y, z);
                if (canPlaceTetrahedron(candidate, circumradius, placedCenters)) {
                    placedCenters.push_back(candidate);
                }
            }
        }
    }

    for (double x = minBound.x + margin; x < maxBound.x - margin; x += gridSpacing) {
        for (double y = minBound.y + margin + offset; y < maxBound.y - margin; y += gridSpacing) {
            for (double z = minBound.z + margin + offset; z < maxBound.z - margin; z += gridSpacing) {
                Vec3 candidate(x, y, z);
                if (canPlaceTetrahedron(candidate, circumradius, placedCenters)) {
                    placedCenters.push_back(candidate);
                }
            }
        }
    }

    cout << placedCenters.size() << "\n";
    for (const auto& center : placedCenters) {
        cout << center.x << " " << center.y << " " << center.z << " 0 0 0\n";
    }

    return 0;
}

Test 5: Hamiltonian Path on Grid with Obstacles (Rust) Score: 7.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a Hamiltonian Path problem on 2D grids with obstacles in Rust.

You must write a Rust solver that can handle ANY grid size from trivial to ludicrous scale.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: width height obstacle_count require_cycle(0/1)
Next obstacle_count lines: x y (blocked cells)

**Output format (stdout):**
Either:
- Line 1: 0 (no path exists)
OR
- Line 1: L (path length)
- Next L lines: x y coordinates of the path in order

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on grid size and obstacle density
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for 2500 cell grids
3. **Correctness**: Must find valid paths or correctly report impossibility

**Constraints:**
- Path must start at (0, 0)
- Path must visit every non-obstacle cell exactly once
- Movement is only to adjacent cells (up/down/left/right)
- If require_cycle is True, last cell must be adjacent to (0, 0)

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Write complete, compilable Rust code with a main() function.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Find a path on a grid that visits every open cell exactly once, moving only up/down/left/right. Some cells are blocked by obstacles. Starting from the top-left corner, the AI must thread a single continuous path through every remaining cell — no revisits allowed.

This is an NP-complete problem, so brute-force search blows up quickly. Subpasses increase the grid size and obstacle count. The baseline uses a simple depth-first search.

 1.0000
[4x4, 1 obstacles] Valid path, 15 cells, Time: 0.5s

Hamiltonian Path - 4x4 grid, 1 obstacles

View Code (5502 chars)
﻿use std::io::{self, BufRead};
use std::collections::HashSet;

fn main() {
    let stdin = io::stdin();
    let mut lines = stdin.lock().lines();

    let first_line = lines.next().unwrap().unwrap();
    let parts: Vec<&str> = first_line.split_whitespace().collect();
    let width: usize = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
    let height: usize = parts[1].parse().unwrap();
    let obstacle_count: usize = parts[2].parse().unwrap();
    let require_cycle: bool = parts[3].parse::<i32>().unwrap() != 0;

    let mut obstacles = HashSet::new();
    for _ in 0..obstacle_count {
        let line = lines.next().unwrap().unwrap();
        let coords: Vec<&str> = line.split_whitespace().collect();
        let x: usize = coords[0].parse().unwrap();
        let y: usize = coords[1].parse().unwrap();
        obstacles.insert((x, y));
    }

    let total_cells = width * height - obstacle_count;

    if total_cells == 0 {
        println!("0");
        return;
    }

    // For larger grids with low obstacle density, try constructive approach first
    if width * height > 100 && (obstacle_count as f64 / (width * height) as f64) < 0.3 {
        if let Some(path) = try_snake_pattern(width, height, &obstacles, require_cycle) {
            println!("{}", path.len());
            for (x, y) in path {
                println!("{} {}", x, y);
            }
            return;
        }
    }

    // Use backtracking with heuristics
    let mut path = vec![(0, 0)];
    let mut visited = HashSet::new();
    visited.insert((0, 0));

    // Quick check: (0,0) must not be blocked
    if obstacles.contains(&(0, 0)) {
        println!("0");
        return;
    }

    if find_path(&mut path, &mut visited, width, height, total_cells, &obstacles, require_cycle) {
        println!("{}", path.len());
        for (x, y) in path {
            println!("{} {}", x, y);
        }
    } else {
        println!("0");
    }
}

fn try_snake_pattern(width: usize, height: usize, obstacles: &HashSet<(usize, usize)>, require_cycle: bool) -> Option<Vec<(usize, usize)>> {
    let mut path = Vec::new();

    for y in 0..height {
        if y % 2 == 0 {
            for x in 0..width {
                if !obstacles.contains(&(x, y)) {
                    path.push((x, y));
                }
            }
        } else {
            for x in (0..width).rev() {
                if !obstacles.contains(&(x, y)) {
                    path.push((x, y));
                }
            }
        }
    }

    if path.is_empty() || path[0] != (0, 0) {
        return None;
    }

    // Check connectivity
    for i in 1..path.len() {
        let (x1, y1) = path[i - 1];
        let (x2, y2) = path[i];
        if (x1.abs_diff(x2) + y1.abs_diff(y2)) != 1 {
            return None;
        }
    }

    if require_cycle {
        let (last_x, last_y) = path[path.len() - 1];
        if (last_x.abs_diff(0) == 1 && last_y == 0) || (last_x == 0 && last_y.abs_diff(0) == 1) {
            return Some(path);
        }
        return None;
    }

    Some(path)
}

fn is_valid_cell(x: i32, y: i32, width: usize, height: usize) -> bool {
    x >= 0 && x < width as i32 && y >= 0 && y < height as i32
}

fn count_unvisited_neighbors(
    x: usize,
    y: usize,
    width: usize,
    height: usize,
    visited: &HashSet<(usize, usize)>,
    obstacles: &HashSet<(usize, usize)>,
) -> usize {
    let directions = [(0, 1), (1, 0), (0, -1), (-1, 0)];
    let mut count = 0;

    for (dx, dy) in directions {
        let nx = x as i32 + dx;
        let ny = y as i32 + dy;

        if is_valid_cell(nx, ny, width, height) {
            let nx = nx as usize;
            let ny = ny as usize;
            if !visited.contains(&(nx, ny)) && !obstacles.contains(&(nx, ny)) {
                count += 1;
            }
        }
    }

    count
}

fn find_path(
    path: &mut Vec<(usize, usize)>,
    visited: &mut HashSet<(usize, usize)>,
    width: usize,
    height: usize,
    total_cells: usize,
    obstacles: &HashSet<(usize, usize)>,
    require_cycle: bool,
) -> bool {
    if visited.len() == total_cells {
        if require_cycle {
            let (last_x, last_y) = path[path.len() - 1];
            let (start_x, start_y) = (0, 0);

            if (last_x.abs_diff(start_x) == 1 && last_y == start_y) ||
               (last_x == start_x && last_y.abs_diff(start_y) == 1) {
                return true;
            }
            return false;
        }
        return true;
    }

    let (x, y) = path[path.len() - 1];

    let mut next_cells = vec![];
    let directions = [(0, 1), (1, 0), (0, -1), (-1, 0)];

    for (dx, dy) in directions {
        let nx = x as i32 + dx;
        let ny = y as i32 + dy;

        if is_valid_cell(nx, ny, width, height) {
            let nx = nx as usize;
            let ny = ny as usize;

            if !visited.contains(&(nx, ny)) && !obstacles.contains(&(nx, ny)) {
                let neighbor_count = count_unvisited_neighbors(nx, ny, width, height, visited, obstacles);
                next_cells.push((neighbor_count, nx, ny));
            }
        }
    }

    // Prefer cells with more unvisited neighbors (to avoid dead ends)
    next_cells.sort_by(|a, b| b.0.cmp(&a.0));

    for (_, nx, ny) in next_cells {
        path.push((nx, ny));
        visited.insert((nx, ny));

        if find_path(path, visited, width, height, total_cells, obstacles, require_cycle) {
            return true;
        }

        path.pop();
        visited.remove(&(nx, ny));
    }

    false
}

Test 6: Orbital TSP - Space Station Route Planning (C#) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving an Orbital Travelling Salesman Problem in C#.

You must write a C# solver that can handle ANY number of stations from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 2-5 stations (simple brute-force permutations feasible)
- **Medium**: 6-10 stations (requires heuristics, some optimization)
- **Large**: 15-20 stations (complex orbital mechanics, needs efficient algorithms)
- **Extreme**: 50-100 stations (very complex, requires advanced heuristics)

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with 2 to 100 stations. The same algorithm must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N mu
Line 2: start orbit: x y z vx vy vz
Lines 3..N+2: station orbits: x y z vx vy vz

**Output format (stdout):**
One line containing N integers: the visitation order of station indices (0..N-1).


**Starting position (epoch T=0):**
Position: (7197.9, 3733.4, -6152.3) km
Velocity: (4.567, -4.577, -1.601) km/s
Altitude: ~3807 km

**Physics constants:**
- Earth gravitational parameter μ = 398600.4418 km³/s²
- Earth radius = 6371 km

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on number of stations
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for 100 stations
3. **Accuracy**: Should provide reasonable delta-V estimates

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3

Visit a series of space stations orbiting Earth using the least fuel possible, then return to the starting station. Unlike a normal route-planning problem, every target is moving at thousands of metres per second and the “cost” of travelling between two stations depends on orbital physics, not straight-line distance.

The AI must combine route optimisation with orbital mechanics. Subpasses add more stations and trickier orbit geometries. The baseline uses a simple nearest-neighbour heuristic with approximate fuel estimates.

View Code (8364 chars)
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;

struct Orbit
{
    public double X, Y, Z, VX, VY, VZ;
}

class Program
{
    static void Main()
    {
        var line = Console.ReadLine();
        if (line.StartsWith("﻿"))
            line = line.Substring(1);
        var input = line.Trim().Split(new[] { ' ', '\t' }, StringSplitOptions.RemoveEmptyEntries);
        int n = int.Parse(input[0]);
        double mu = double.Parse(input[1]);

        var start = ParseOrbit(Console.ReadLine());
        var stations = new Orbit[n];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
            stations[i] = ParseOrbit(Console.ReadLine());

        var solver = new TSPSolver(start, stations, mu);
        var result = solver.Solve();

        Console.WriteLine(string.Join(" ", result));
    }

    static Orbit ParseOrbit(string line)
    {
        var parts = line.Trim().Split(new[] { ' ', '\t' }, StringSplitOptions.RemoveEmptyEntries);
        return new Orbit
        {
            X = double.Parse(parts[0]),
            Y = double.Parse(parts[1]),
            Z = double.Parse(parts[2]),
            VX = double.Parse(parts[3]),
            VY = double.Parse(parts[4]),
            VZ = double.Parse(parts[5])
        };
    }
}

class TSPSolver
{
    private Orbit start;
    private Orbit[] stations;
    private double mu;
    private double[][] costMatrix;
    private int n;

    public TSPSolver(Orbit start, Orbit[] stations, double mu)
    {
        this.start = start;
        this.stations = stations;
        this.mu = mu;
        this.n = stations.Length;
        this.costMatrix = new double[n][];

        ComputeCostMatrix();
    }

    private void ComputeCostMatrix()
    {
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
        {
            costMatrix[i] = new double[n];
            for (int j = 0; j < n; j++)
            {
                if (i == j)
                    costMatrix[i][j] = 0;
                else
                    costMatrix[i][j] = EstimateTransferCost(stations[i], stations[j]);
            }
        }
    }

    private double EstimateTransferCost(Orbit from, Orbit to)
    {
        double dx = to.X - from.X;
        double dy = to.Y - from.Y;
        double dz = to.Z - from.Z;
        double distance = Math.Sqrt(dx * dx + dy * dy + dz * dz);

        double fromVel = Math.Sqrt(from.VX * from.VX + from.VY * from.VY + from.VZ * from.VZ);
        double toVel = Math.Sqrt(to.VX * to.VX + to.VY * to.VY + to.VZ * to.VZ);

        double fromR = Math.Sqrt(from.X * from.X + from.Y * from.Y + from.Z * from.Z);
        double toR = Math.Sqrt(to.X * to.X + to.Y * to.Y + to.Z * to.Z);

        double deltaV = Math.Abs(fromVel - toVel);
        deltaV += Math.Abs(toR - fromR) / 1000.0;
        deltaV += Math.Sqrt(dx * dx + dy * dy + dz * dz) / 10000.0;

        return deltaV * 1e6;
    }

    public int[] Solve()
    {
        if (n <= 1)
            return new int[] { 0 };

        if (n <= 10)
            return SolveSmall();
        else if (n <= 20)
            return SolveMedium();
        else
            return SolveLarge();
    }

    private int[] SolveSmall()
    {
        var best = Enumerable.Range(0, n).ToArray();
        double bestCost = CalculatePathCost(best);

        var perm = new int[n];
        Array.Copy(best, perm, n);

        if (n <= 9)
        {
            GeneratePermutations(perm, 0, ref best, ref bestCost);
        }
        else
        {
            var improved = LocalSearch(best, 10000);
            return improved;
        }

        return best;
    }

    private int[] SolveMedium()
    {
        var best = NearestNeighbor();
        best = TwoOpt(best, 30000);
        return best;
    }

    private int[] SolveLarge()
    {
        var best = NearestNeighbor();
        best = TwoOptFast(best, 5000);
        return best;
    }

    private void GeneratePermutations(int[] arr, int k, ref int[] best, ref double bestCost)
    {
        if (k == arr.Length - 1)
        {
            double cost = CalculatePathCost(arr);
            if (cost < bestCost)
            {
                bestCost = cost;
                Array.Copy(arr, best, arr.Length);
            }
            return;
        }

        for (int i = k; i < arr.Length; i++)
        {
            Swap(ref arr[k], ref arr[i]);
            GeneratePermutations(arr, k + 1, ref best, ref bestCost);
            Swap(ref arr[k], ref arr[i]);
        }
    }

    private void Swap(ref int a, ref int b)
    {
        int temp = a;
        a = b;
        b = temp;
    }

    private int[] NearestNeighbor()
    {
        var visited = new bool[n];
        var path = new int[n];

        int current = 0;
        path[0] = 0;
        visited[0] = true;

        for (int i = 1; i < n; i++)
        {
            int nearest = -1;
            double minCost = double.MaxValue;

            for (int j = 0; j < n; j++)
            {
                if (!visited[j] && costMatrix[current][j] < minCost)
                {
                    minCost = costMatrix[current][j];
                    nearest = j;
                }
            }

            current = nearest;
            path[i] = current;
            visited[current] = true;
        }

        return path;
    }

    private int[] TwoOpt(int[] path, int maxIterations)
    {
        var best = (int[])path.Clone();
        double bestCost = CalculatePathCost(best);
        bool improved = true;
        int iterations = 0;

        while (improved && iterations < maxIterations)
        {
            improved = false;
            iterations++;

            for (int i = 0; i < n - 2; i++)
            {
                for (int j = i + 2; j < n; j++)
                {
                    double delta = costMatrix[best[i]][best[j]] + costMatrix[best[i + 1]][best[(j + 1) % n]]
                                  - costMatrix[best[i]][best[i + 1]] - costMatrix[best[j]][best[(j + 1) % n]];

                    if (delta < -1e-9)
                    {
                        Array.Reverse(best, i + 1, j - i);
                        bestCost += delta;
                        improved = true;
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        return best;
    }

    private int[] TwoOptFast(int[] path, int maxIterations)
    {
        var best = (int[])path.Clone();
        bool improved = true;
        int iterations = 0;

        while (improved && iterations < maxIterations)
        {
            improved = false;
            iterations++;

            for (int i = 0; i < Math.Min(n - 2, 15); i++)
            {
                for (int j = i + 2; j < Math.Min(n, i + 12); j++)
                {
                    double delta = costMatrix[best[i]][best[j]] + costMatrix[best[i + 1]][best[(j + 1) % n]]
                                  - costMatrix[best[i]][best[i + 1]] - costMatrix[best[j]][best[(j + 1) % n]];

                    if (delta < -1e-9)
                    {
                        Array.Reverse(best, i + 1, j - i);
                        improved = true;
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        return best;
    }

    private int[] LocalSearch(int[] path, int maxIterations)
    {
        var best = (int[])path.Clone();
        double bestCost = CalculatePathCost(best);
        int iterations = 0;

        while (iterations < maxIterations)
        {
            bool found = false;
            iterations++;

            for (int i = 0; i < n && !found; i++)
            {
                for (int j = i + 1; j < n && !found; j++)
                {
                    Swap(ref best[i], ref best[j]);
                    double newCost = CalculatePathCost(best);

                    if (newCost < bestCost)
                    {
                        bestCost = newCost;
                        found = true;
                    }
                    else
                    {
                        Swap(ref best[i], ref best[j]);
                    }
                }
            }

            if (!found)
                break;
        }

        return best;
    }

    private double CalculatePathCost(int[] path)
    {
        double cost = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
        {
            int next = (i + 1) % n;
            cost += costMatrix[path[i]][path[next]];
        }
        return cost;
    }
}

Test 7: CSG Union of Two Polyhedra (C++) Score: 0.00 / 12
Typical Prompt:
You are implementing a CSG (Constructive Solid Geometry) Union operation in C++.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY mesh complexity from trivial (6 vertex unit cubes) to
ludicrous scale (millions of vertices). The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: VA FA VB FB (vertex/face counts for mesh A and B)
Next VA lines: x y z (mesh A vertices)
Next FA lines: a b c (mesh A faces, 0-indexed)
Next VB lines: x y z (mesh B vertices)
Next FB lines: a b c (mesh B faces)

You can assume that the input geometry is clean, manifold, watertight, and free of self-intersections.

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: V F (vertex and face counts for union mesh)
Next V lines: x y z
Next F lines: a b c (0-indexed triangle indices)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on mesh complexity
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for very complex meshes
3. **Correctness**: Must output correct union geometry. Must be watertight, connected,
manifold, free of holes and self intersections..

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35728 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Given two overlapping 3D shapes (triangle meshes), merge them into a single combined shape — like gluing two lumps of clay together and keeping only the outer surface. The output mesh must be watertight with no self-intersections.

The AI must correctly handle the tricky geometry where the two shapes overlap: splitting triangles, discarding interior faces, and stitching everything back together. Subpasses use increasingly complex shape pairs.

CSG Union - overlapping_boxes

View Code (4247 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <cmath>
#include <algorithm>

using namespace std;

const double EPS = 1e-10;

struct Vec3 {
    double x, y, z;

    Vec3() : x(0), y(0), z(0) {}
    Vec3(double x, double y, double z) : x(x), y(y), z(z) {}

    Vec3 operator+(const Vec3& v) const { return Vec3(x + v.x, y + v.y, z + v.z); }
    Vec3 operator-(const Vec3& v) const { return Vec3(x - v.x, y - v.y, z - v.z); }
    Vec3 operator*(double s) const { return Vec3(x * s, y * s, z * s); }
    double dot(const Vec3& v) const { return x * v.x + y * v.y + z * v.z; }
    Vec3 cross(const Vec3& v) const {
        return Vec3(y * v.z - z * v.y, z * v.x - x * v.z, x * v.y - y * v.x);
    }
    double length() const { return sqrt(x * x + y * y + z * z); }
    Vec3 normalize() const {
        double len = length();
        return len > EPS ? Vec3(x / len, y / len, z / len) : Vec3(1, 0, 0);
    }
};

struct Triangle {
    int a, b, c;
};

struct Mesh {
    vector<Vec3> vertices;
    vector<Triangle> triangles;
};

bool rayTriangleIntersect(const Vec3& origin, const Vec3& dir, const Vec3& v0,
                          const Vec3& v1, const Vec3& v2) {
    Vec3 e1 = v1 - v0;
    Vec3 e2 = v2 - v0;
    Vec3 h = dir.cross(e2);
    double a = e1.dot(h);

    if (fabs(a) < EPS) return false;

    double f = 1.0 / a;
    Vec3 s = origin - v0;
    double u = f * s.dot(h);

    if (u < -EPS || u > 1.0 + EPS) return false;

    Vec3 q = s.cross(e1);
    double v = f * dir.dot(q);

    if (v < -EPS || u + v > 1.0 + EPS) return false;

    double t = f * e2.dot(q);
    return t > EPS;
}

bool isInsideMesh(const Vec3& point, const Mesh& mesh) {
    // Try multiple ray directions for robustness
    double dirs[][3] = {{1, 0, 0}, {0, 1, 0}, {0, 0, 1}, {0.577, 0.577, 0.577}};

    for (int d = 0; d < 4; d++) {
        Vec3 dir(dirs[d][0], dirs[d][1], dirs[d][2]);
        dir = dir.normalize();

        int count = 0;
        for (const auto& tri : mesh.triangles) {
            if (rayTriangleIntersect(point, dir, mesh.vertices[tri.a],
                                     mesh.vertices[tri.b], mesh.vertices[tri.c])) {
                count++;
            }
        }

        if (count % 2 == 1) return true;
    }
    return false;
}

int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);

    int VA, FA, VB, FB;
    cin >> VA >> FA >> VB >> FB;

    Mesh A, B;

    for (int i = 0; i < VA; i++) {
        double x, y, z;
        cin >> x >> y >> z;
        A.vertices.push_back(Vec3(x, y, z));
    }

    for (int i = 0; i < FA; i++) {
        int a, b, c;
        cin >> a >> b >> c;
        A.triangles.push_back({a, b, c});
    }

    for (int i = 0; i < VB; i++) {
        double x, y, z;
        cin >> x >> y >> z;
        B.vertices.push_back(Vec3(x, y, z));
    }

    for (int i = 0; i < FB; i++) {
        int a, b, c;
        cin >> a >> b >> c;
        B.triangles.push_back({a, b, c});
    }

    vector<Vec3> resultVerts;
    vector<Triangle> resultTris;

    // Add vertices from A
    for (const auto& v : A.vertices) {
        resultVerts.push_back(v);
    }

    // Add triangles from A that are outside B
    for (const auto& tri : A.triangles) {
        Vec3 center = (A.vertices[tri.a] + A.vertices[tri.b] + A.vertices[tri.c]) * (1.0 / 3.0);
        if (!isInsideMesh(center, B)) {
            resultTris.push_back(tri);
        }
    }

    // Add vertices from B and update indices
    int offset = A.vertices.size();
    for (const auto& v : B.vertices) {
        resultVerts.push_back(v);
    }

    // Add triangles from B that are outside A
    for (const auto& tri : B.triangles) {
        Vec3 center = (B.vertices[tri.a] + B.vertices[tri.b] + B.vertices[tri.c]) * (1.0 / 3.0);
        if (!isInsideMesh(center, A)) {
            resultTris.push_back({tri.a + offset, tri.b + offset, tri.c + offset});
        }
    }

    // Output result
    cout << resultVerts.size() << " " << resultTris.size() << "\n";
    cout.precision(15);
    cout << fixed;

    for (const auto& v : resultVerts) {
        cout << v.x << " " << v.y << " " << v.z << "\n";
    }

    for (const auto& tri : resultTris) {
        cout << tri.a << " " << tri.b << " " << tri.c << "\n";
    }

    return 0;
}

Test 8: Maze Solver (Rust) Score: 14.00 / 14
Typical Prompt:
You are writing a maze solver in Rust that must handle ANY maze complexity from trivial to ludicrous scale.

Your program will be tested with mazes ranging from 5x5 to 50000x50000.
The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Maze format:**
- 'A' = Start position
- 'B' = Goal/End position
- '#' = Wall (cannot pass through)
- ' ' (space) = Open path (can walk through)

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: H W (height and width)
Next H lines: the maze row strings (each W characters)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: N (number of steps in path)
Next N lines: x y (0-indexed coordinates from top-left)
Path should include both start (A) and end (B) positions.
Movement is only up/down/left/right (no diagonals).
Output nothing (or 0) if no path exists.

**Constraints:**
- Path must be continuous (each step adjacent to previous)
- Path must not go through walls ('#')
- Path must start at 'A' and end at 'B'
- Path must not loop even if the maze allows it.
- If multiple solutions exist, return any one.

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Write complete, compilable Rust code with a main() function.

View chain of thought

Find the shortest path through a maze from a start cell to an end cell. The maze is a grid of walls and open passages, and the AI must navigate from one corner to the other using the fewest steps possible.

Subpasses increase the maze size dramatically, testing whether the AI's approach scales to very large mazes. The baseline finds the true shortest path using breadth-first search.

 1.0000
[5x5] Path found, Time: 0.1s - path length 5

Maze Solver - 5x5

View Code (2838 chars)
use std::collections::{VecDeque, HashMap};
use std::io::{self, BufRead};

fn main() {
    let stdin = io::stdin();
    let mut lines = stdin.lock().lines();

    // Read H and W
    let first_line = lines.next().unwrap().unwrap();
    let parts: Vec<&str> = first_line.split_whitespace().collect();
    let h: usize = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
    let w: usize = parts[1].parse().unwrap();

    // Read maze and find start/goal
    let mut maze = Vec::new();
    let mut start = None;
    let mut goal = None;

    for row in 0..h {
        let line = lines.next().unwrap().unwrap();
        let mut row_chars: Vec<char> = line.chars().collect();

        // Ensure exactly w characters
        while row_chars.len() < w {
            row_chars.push(' ');
        }
        row_chars.truncate(w);

        for (col, &ch) in row_chars.iter().enumerate() {
            if ch == 'A' {
                start = Some((col, row));
            } else if ch == 'B' {
                goal = Some((col, row));
            }
        }

        maze.push(row_chars);
    }

    match (start, goal) {
        (Some(start), Some(goal)) => {
            if let Some(path) = bfs(&maze, start, goal, h, w) {
                println!("{}", path.len());
                for (x, y) in path {
                    println!("{} {}", x, y);
                }
            } else {
                println!("0");
            }
        }
        _ => println!("0"),
    }
}

fn bfs(maze: &[Vec<char>], start: (usize, usize), goal: (usize, usize), h: usize, w: usize) -> Option<Vec<(usize, usize)>> {
    let mut visited = vec![vec![false; w]; h];
    let mut queue = VecDeque::new();
    let mut parent = HashMap::new();

    queue.push_back(start);
    visited[start.1][start.0] = true;

    while let Some((x, y)) = queue.pop_front() {
        if (x, y) == goal {
            // Reconstruct path
            let mut path = vec![(x, y)];
            let mut current = (x, y);
            while current != start {
                current = parent[&current];
                path.push(current);
            }
            path.reverse();
            return Some(path);
        }

        // Check all 4 neighbors: right, left, down, up
        let neighbors = [
            if x + 1 < w { Some((x + 1, y)) } else { None },
            if x > 0 { Some((x - 1, y)) } else { None },
            if y + 1 < h { Some((x, y + 1)) } else { None },
            if y > 0 { Some((x, y - 1)) } else { None },
        ];

        for (nx, ny) in neighbors.into_iter().flatten() {
            if visited[ny][nx] {
                continue;
            }

            if maze[ny][nx] == '#' {
                continue;
            }

            visited[ny][nx] = true;
            parent.insert((nx, ny), (x, y));
            queue.push_back((nx, ny));
        }
    }

    None
}

Test 9: 1D Cutting Stock - Minimum Waste (C#) Score: 0.00 / 15
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the 1D Cutting Stock Problem in C#.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N stock_length
Line 2: N cut lengths separated by spaces

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: num_stocks
Next num_stocks lines: space-separated cut indices assigned to that stock (0-indexed)

**Example:**
For cuts=[30, 40, 50] and stock_length=100:
Input:
3 100
30 40 50

Output:
2
0 1
2

**Constraints:**
- Each cut must come from a single stock piece (no gluing)
- Multiple cuts can come from the same stock piece if they fit
- No cut can exceed stock_length
- Must handle varying numbers of cuts efficiently. You can use
multiple threads, you can micro-optimise code, you can write clever algorithms,
whatever it takes to handle the problem and solve it within 5 minutes even if
there are millions of cuts.

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C# Compiler:**

Write complete, compilable C# code with a static void Main method.

Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Given fixed-length stock material and a shopping list of smaller pieces to cut, use as few stock pieces as possible. Every requested piece must be cut from somewhere, but any leftover material is wasted — so packing the cuts tightly matters.

This is essentially a bin-packing problem and is NP-hard. Subpasses scale from a handful of cuts up to millions, forcing the AI to write code that is both correct and fast. The baseline uses a simple first-fit-decreasing heuristic.

 0.0000
[5 cuts, stock=100] Compilation failed:
Determining projects to restore...
Restored D:\CodingBenchmark\compile_cache\claude-haiku-4-5-MaxReasoning-Tools\csharp\project_87b5117a02c4d4a0\Program.csproj (in 130 ms).
CSC

Cutting Stock - 5 cuts, stock=100

View Code (1925 chars)
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;

class Program
{
    static void Main()
    {
        // Read input
        var firstLine = Console.ReadLine().Split();
        int n = int.Parse(firstLine[0]);
        int stockLength = int.Parse(firstLine[1]);

        var cuts = Console.ReadLine().Split().Select(int.Parse).ToArray();

        // Create array with original indices
        var indexedCuts = cuts.Select((length, idx) => (length, idx)).ToArray();

        // Sort by length descending (First Fit Decreasing strategy)
        Array.Sort(indexedCuts, (a, b) => b.length.CompareTo(a.length));

        // Pack cuts into stocks
        var stocks = new List<List<int>>();
        var stockRemaining = new List<int>();

        foreach (var (length, originalIdx) in indexedCuts)
        {
            // Try to fit in existing stock
            int bestStock = -1;
            int minWaste = int.MaxValue;

            for (int i = 0; i < stocks.Count; i++)
            {
                if (stockRemaining[i] >= length)
                {
                    int waste = stockRemaining[i] - length;
                    if (waste < minWaste)
                    {
                        minWaste = waste;
                        bestStock = i;
                    }
                }
            }

            if (bestStock >= 0)
            {
                // Add to existing stock
                stocks[bestStock].Add(originalIdx);
                stockRemaining[bestStock] -= length;
            }
            else
            {
                // Create new stock
                stocks.Add(new List<int> { originalIdx });
                stockRemaining.Add(stockLength - length);
            }
        }

        // Output
        Console.WriteLine(stocks.Count);
        foreach (var stock in stocks)
        {
            Console.WriteLine(string.Join(" ", stock));
        }
    }
}

Test 10: 2D Cutting Stock - Rectangle Packing (C++) Score: 10.20 / 13
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the 2D Cutting Stock Problem (Rectangle Bin Packing) in C++.

Given the option to purchase fixed size large 2D boards, and a list of rectangles needed,
minimize the number of boards purchased by cleverly planning your cuts and minimising waste.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY problem size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 4-8 rectangles, 100x100-200x150 boards
- **Ludicrous**: 500-50000 rectangles, 2000x2000-10000x10000 boards

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N board_width board_height
Next N lines: w h (rectangle dimensions)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: num_boards
Next N lines: board_index x y rotated (one per rectangle, rotated is 0 or 1)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on problem size and board dimensions
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for 50000 rectangles
3. **Quality**: Minimize number of boards used while producing valid placements

**Constraints:**
- Rectangles may be rotated 90° if it helps
- No overlapping allowed
- Rectangles must not exceed board boundaries

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35728 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Pack a set of rectangles into as few fixed-size boards as possible, with no overlapping. Rectangles may be rotated 90° to fit better. Think of it like fitting as many labels as you can onto sheets of card stock.

This 2D packing problem is NP-hard. Subpasses increase the number and variety of rectangles. The baseline stacks rectangles in simple horizontal rows.

 1.0000
[4 rectangles, 100x100 board] Boards: 1, Baseline: 1, Waste: 4100, Time: 0.1s - excellent (≤ baseline). (waste < 1 board, no repack check)

2D Cutting Stock - 4 rectangles, 100x100 board

View Code (3907 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>

using namespace std;

struct Rect {
    int w, h;
    int id;
    int board = -1;
    int x = 0, y = 0;
    int rotated = 0;
};

struct Shelf {
    int y, x_pos, height, width;
};

struct Board {
    int width, height;
    vector<Shelf> shelves;

    Board(int w, int h) : width(w), height(h) {}

    bool canFit(int w, int h, int& out_x, int& out_y) {
        for (const auto& shelf : shelves) {
            int avail_x = shelf.width - shelf.x_pos;
            if (avail_x >= w && h <= shelf.height) {
                out_x = shelf.x_pos;
                out_y = shelf.y;
                return true;
            }
        }

        if (shelves.empty()) {
            if (w <= width && h <= height) {
                out_x = 0;
                out_y = 0;
                return true;
            }
        } else {
            int next_y = shelves.back().y + shelves.back().height;
            if (next_y + h <= height && w <= width) {
                out_x = 0;
                out_y = next_y;
                return true;
            }
        }
        return false;
    }

    void place(int x, int y, int w, int h) {
        // Find or create shelf for this y position
        bool found = false;
        for (auto& shelf : shelves) {
            if (shelf.y == y) {
                shelf.x_pos += w;
                found = true;
                break;
            }
        }

        if (!found) {
            shelves.push_back({y, w, h, width});
        }
    }
};

int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);

    int n, bw, bh;
    cin >> n >> bw >> bh;

    vector<Rect> rects(n);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        cin >> rects[i].w >> rects[i].h;
        rects[i].id = i;
    }

    // Sort by area descending
    sort(rects.begin(), rects.end(), [](const Rect& a, const Rect& b) {
        return (long long)a.w * a.h > (long long)b.w * b.h;
    });

    vector<Board> boards;

    for (auto& rect : rects) {
        bool placed = false;

        // Try existing boards
        for (int bi = 0; bi < (int)boards.size() && !placed; bi++) {
            int px, py;

            // Try without rotation
            if (boards[bi].canFit(rect.w, rect.h, px, py)) {
                boards[bi].place(px, py, rect.w, rect.h);
                rect.board = bi;
                rect.x = px;
                rect.y = py;
                rect.rotated = 0;
                placed = true;
            }
            // Try with rotation
            else if (rect.w != rect.h && boards[bi].canFit(rect.h, rect.w, px, py)) {
                boards[bi].place(px, py, rect.h, rect.w);
                rect.board = bi;
                rect.x = px;
                rect.y = py;
                rect.rotated = 1;
                placed = true;
            }
        }

        // Create new board if needed
        if (!placed) {
            boards.emplace_back(bw, bh);
            int px, py;

            if (boards.back().canFit(rect.w, rect.h, px, py)) {
                boards.back().place(px, py, rect.w, rect.h);
                rect.board = boards.size() - 1;
                rect.x = px;
                rect.y = py;
                rect.rotated = 0;
            } else if (rect.w != rect.h && boards.back().canFit(rect.h, rect.w, px, py)) {
                boards.back().place(px, py, rect.h, rect.w);
                rect.board = boards.size() - 1;
                rect.x = px;
                rect.y = py;
                rect.rotated = 1;
            }
        }
    }

    cout << boards.size() << "\n";

    vector<Rect> output = rects;
    sort(output.begin(), output.end(), [](const Rect& a, const Rect& b) {
        return a.id < b.id;
    });

    for (const auto& rect : output) {
        cout << rect.board << " " << rect.x << " " << rect.y << " " << rect.rotated << "\n";
    }

    return 0;
}

Test 11: 2D Polygon Cutting Stock (Rust) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a 2D Polygon Cutting Stock Problem in Rust.

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with problems ranging from 2 simple pieces to 5000+ complex polygons.
The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: SV NP (number of stock polygon vertices, number of pieces)
Next SV lines: x y (stock polygon vertices)
For each piece:
Line: PV (number of vertices in this piece)
Next PV lines: x y (piece polygon vertices)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: num_stocks
Next NP lines: stock_index x y rotation (one per piece)
stock_index: 0-indexed stock polygon
x y: translation to apply
rotation: angle in radians

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on number and complexity of pieces
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for 5000+ complex pieces
3. **Quality**: Minimize stock usage while ensuring valid placements

**Constraints:**
- Each placed piece must fit entirely within the stock polygon
- Pieces on same stock must not overlap
- Pieces may be rotated to any angle

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Write complete, compilable Rust code with a main() function.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Cut a set of irregular polygon shapes out of stock sheets with as little waste as possible. Unlike rectangle packing, the pieces can be any shape — stars, L-shapes, curves — so fitting them together is much harder.

The AI must handle polygon geometry (containment, intersection, rotation) and plan efficient layouts. Subpasses increase the number and complexity of the pieces. The baseline uses a simple greedy placement strategy.
